content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
December 23,
1993
Raymond M.
Moore
2619 Latham Drive
Sacramento,
CA
95864—7141
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—402
Dear Mr. Moore:
This is in response to your request for advice under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”.)
1
! Since you are merely seeking guidance regarding your
conduct under the Act, your request is being considered a request
for informal assistance.
2
!
OUESTION
How will the conflict-of—interest provisions of the Act
affect your participation in decisions as an employee of the
-
Department of Parks and Recreation where the decisions may affect
your private sector employer, the J.R.
Schneider Co.?
CONCLUSION
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act will prohibit
you from making, or participating in the making of,
any
governmental decision which will have a material financial effect
on your private sector employer, J.R.
Schneider Co.
In addition,
you may not use your official position with the State of
California to influence a decision of any other goverrunental
entity with regard to decisions that may affect J.R.
Schneider Co.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the reguestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—93—402
Page 2
FACTS
You are a state employee currently employed as an Associate
Mechanical Engineer with the Department of Parks and Recreation.
Your position is not designated in the department’s Conflict of
Interest Code as one requiring the disclosure of your economic
interests.
Your duties with the state include on-site investigations,
engineering designs, and writing specifications for public works
contracts of a wide variety of project types, including water
treatment systems.
As of November 1,
1993, you reduced your work schedule with
the state to half—time so that you could work as a sales
representative for the J.R. Schneider Co.
(“Schneider”), a
manufacturer of water filtration equipment.
In your position with
Schneider,
you make contacts with persons in cities, counties,
and
federal government agencies, private water purveyors and private
engineering consultants.
You are concerned about conflict—of—interest issues which may
arise as a result of your employment with J.R. Schneider,
Co.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in, or using their official position toinfluence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate
family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.


Advice File No.
1—93—402
Page 3
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of thin section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103
The term “public official” means every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048.)
Although you are not specifically designated in
the Conflict of Interest Code for the Department of Parks and
Recreation,
you are a public official subject to the conflict-of—
interest provisions of the Act.
“Income” as used in Section 87103(c) means a payment
received, including but not limited to any salary, wage, advance,
dividend, interest, rent, or proceeds from any sale.
3 I If you
receive $250 or more in salary or other income from Schneider,
Schneider would be an economic interest to you under Section
87103(c).
In addition, your status as an employee of Schneider would
also be an economic interest under Section 87103 (e).
Accordingly, as a public official, you may not make, participate
in making, or attempt to use your official position to influence a
governmental decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable
4
! material financial effect, distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally,
on you,
on a member of your
Income does not include salary and reimbursement for expenses
from a state, local, or federal government.
(Section 82030.)
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a. substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Advice File No.
1—93—402
Page 4
immediate family, or on any of your economic interests specified
in Section 87103 above, which would include the J.R.
Schneider Co.
In your letter you stated that your duties include on-site
investigations, engineering designs and specification writing for
contracts which include water treatment systems.
You indicated
that if Schneider were selected for use, your supervisor would
make that decision.
However the conflict—of—interest provisions
also prohibit you from participating in the making and influencing
a governmental decision as well.
The phrase “making a governmental decision” means that you
may not vote on a matter,
commit the department to any course of
action, or enter into any contractual agreement on behalf of the
department.
(Regulation 18700 (b) . )
“Participating in a
governmental decision” means while acting within the authority of
your position, you:
(1)
Negotiate, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or
(2)
Advise or make recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 18700(c).
For example, if your duties require you to recommend a
manufacturer or supplier of equipment, without intervening
substantive review of your recommendations,
you are participating
in the making of a governmental decision.
You are also
participating in the making of decisions if you recommend specific
types of equipment to be used in your designs or contracts.
By
doing so, arguably you could limit the type of equipment under
your specifications to only those which Schneider may provide.
Accordingly, unless there is some intervening substantive
review of your recommendations, your employment with Schneider may
prohibit you from performing your normal daily duties with the
department.
You may not participate in recommendations if such
recommendations will have a material financial effect on
Schneider.


Advice File No.
1—93—402
Page 5
By intervening substantive review, we mean that there is
another level of thorough review of your designs or contract
specifications before the work product goes to the final
decisionmaker for approval.
The concept of “independent
substantive review” is narrowly applied.
For -imple, the
Commission determined the exception was not applicable where
decisions are merely “reviewed” by the governmental agency and the
review by the agency is in reliance on the data or analysis
prepared by the employee without checking it independently.
(Kaplan Advice Letter,
No.
A—82—108.)
Using Your Position to Influence a Decision
In addition to making or participating in the making of
governmental decisions, you may not use your official position in
an attempt to influence any governmental decision.
With regard to
a decision which is before your agency, you are attempting to use
your official position to influence the decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, you contact, appear before,
or otherwise attempt to influence, any member, officer,
employee
or consultant of the agency.
Attempts to influence are not
limited to appearances or contacts on behalf of a business entity,
client, or customer.
In addition to making attempts to influence decisions of your
own agency, you may not use your official position to influence
the decision of any other governmental agency.
You may not act,
or purport to act,
on behalf of, or as the representative of, the
Department of Parks and Recreation before any other governmental
agency.
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)
You. may not recommend to-a governmental entity that it use
supplies or equipment of Schneider and claim to be doing so as a
representative of the Department, or to be endorsing Schneider’s
products on behalf of the Department.
Material Financial Effect on Schneider
If you determine that any decision you participate in will
affect Schneider,
you must next’determine if the decision will
have a “material financial effect” on Schneider.
Commission
Regulations 18702.1 and 18702.2
(enclosed) define “material
financial effect” on a business entity source of income.
Regulation 18702.1 determines material financial effect when
Schneider is directly
5
! affected by your recommendations.
5/
A business entity is directly involved in a decision when that
entity initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim,
appeal, or similar request or is
a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning
the decision before the official or the official’s agency.
An
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
business entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
-


idvice File No.
1—93-402
Page 6
Regulation 18702.2 sets out the thresholds for materiality when
Schneider would be indirectly affected by your recommendations.
Since there is no decision before you at this time, we suggest you
contact the Commission when you have a specific decision pending
before you.
Revolving Door Provisions
In addition to the general conflict—of—interest provisions
discussed above, if you terminate your position with the State of
California and work full time in the private sector, there are
certain revolving door provisions which apply to you.
I have
enclosed some letters which describe the revolving door
provisions.
1 hope all of this information was helpful to you and
provided some guidelines for you to follow.
If you have any
additional questions, please feel free to give the Commission’s
Legal Division a call at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
Geneal Counsel
(
SC!JET!jt
Enclosures
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

governmental decision which will have a material financial effect
on your private sector employer, J.R.
Schneider Co.
In addition,
you may not use your official position with the State of
California to influence a decision of any other goverrunental
entity with regard to decisions that may affect J.R.
Schneider Co.
1;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329 (c) (3) . )
 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—93—402

Co.

affect your participation in decisions as an employee of the
-
Department of Parks and Recreation where the decisions may affect
your private sector employer, the J.R.
Schneider Co.?

CONCLUSION: The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act will prohibit
you from making, or participating in the making of,
any
governmental decision which will have a material financial effect
on your private sector employer, J.R.
Schneider Co.
In addition,
you may not use your official position with the State of
California to influence a decision of any other goverrunental
entity with regard to decisions that may affect J.R.
Schneider Co.
